events.text: {{characters.alea.name}} lay on the ground outside the corridor entrance.

They returned after the combat sounds ceased. Not everyone agreed to return. {{characters.lyra.name}} was the first to stop, to turn, to walk back. {{characters.soren.name}} tried to hold her back—it didn't work. {{characters.elias.name}} didn't try. The three of them ended up back at the corridor entrance together.
Face up, lying on the fire-blackened ground. Around her lay pieces of destroyed Solmaris machinery—the price she had paid, paid with herself, enough time and distance for three people to escape.

Her modular systems were completely destroyed. The electromagnetic baton still gripped tightly in her hand, its thermal battery burned out on its final discharge. The holographic blade could no longer regenerate, and the blade itself was snapped clean in the middle.
{{characters.lyra.name}} walked over and knelt beside her.
The body had no neural connection signals. Sync rate detection read zero. Not low—zero. The emptiness where even detection itself no longer existed.

When a body sustains damage beyond a certain point, the soul can no longer remain attached and automatically detaches. If the Ark has recovery equipment, the soul can be captured, transferred to a temporary container, waiting for the next body.
But there was no recovery equipment in the Wasteland. There was nothing in the Wasteland.
A freed soul only had minutes of existence in open air before it would scatter, dissipate, like warm breath on a cold winter day, spreading into the air, leaving no trace.

The expression was peaceful. No furrowed brow, no downturned mouth. Just peace—much like it had been when she was alive. That unshakeable calm that pressed everything down to the deepest level. Only now, there was nothing beneath that calm.

She reached out and gently touched that hand. The back of the hand was cool, but not yet fully cold. There was still a little warmth, a tiny bit, like the last ember before the wind comes, still there.

{{characters.lyra.name}} didn't speak. She suddenly understood in that moment that something had truly, completely ended. Not something that would get another chance later, not something that still existed somewhere else. Complete. A direction had disappeared. Looking toward that direction, there was nothing left.
She held the life nameplate. The metal was cold now, as cold as the Wasteland air itself, carrying none of the warmth she had felt before.

She knelt there without crying. Not because she didn't want to, but because she had fallen into a place deeper than tears. Crying was something that happened on the surface. She was beyond that now, deeper than tears could ever reach.
